narrative.intro: You've landed at John Paul II Kraków–Balice. Here's the fast start—no tourist traps, no exchange rate scams, just the moves that make Poland's former royal capital feel navigable from your first hour inside the Planty ring.

arrival.title: From the Airport
arrival.content:
**Airport → city:** John Paul II Kraków–Balice (KRK) sits ~11 km west of center. The **SKA1 airport train** runs to **Kraków Główny** (main station) in about **20 minutes for 20 PLN**—buy from a machine or tap contactless onboard, and walk the covered link from the terminal to the platform. It's the cleanest, most reliable option.

**Buses:** The express **bus 300** (and the more local **209**) reach central stops for just **6 PLN**, with the **night bus 902** covering the overnight gap. Buy and validate a ticket onboard.

**Taxis & rideshare:** A **Bolt or official taxi** to the Old Town runs roughly **70-110 PLN**, about 25-30 minutes depending on traffic. Use the **Bolt or Uber apps** so the fare is fixed up front—skip anyone approaching you in the terminal.

**Pro tip:** Get **Polish złoty (PLN)** from a bank ATM at the airport, not a Euronet kiosk or exchange booth. Use a debit card with no foreign transaction fees, and always decline 'dynamic currency conversion.'

gettingAround.title: Getting Around
gettingAround.content:
**Walking covers the core.** The **Old Town (Stare Miasto)** is compact and ringed by the **Planty** park—you can cross it end to end in 15 minutes. **Rynek Główny** (Europe's largest medieval market square), Wawel, and the gate to Kazimierz are all an easy stroll apart.

**Trams & buses (MPK)** handle longer hops—out to Kazimierz, Podgórze, the Schindler Factory, or the main station. Buy a **single ticket** from the machine onboard or at stops and **validate it immediately**; inspectors do check. A 24- or 72-hour pass pays off if you're moving around.

**No car needed.** The Old Town is largely pedestrianized, parking is a headache, and everything central is walkable. Save driving for nothing—even day trips run better by train or organized transport.

money.title: Money & Payments
money.content:
**Polish Złoty (PLN), not Euro.** Poland is in the EU but keeps its own currency. A few tourist spots quote Euro at poor rates—pay in PLN everywhere.

**Cards work nearly everywhere**—Visa and Mastercard, contactless and Apple/Google Pay, are accepted at restaurants, shops, and even market stalls. Tap-to-pay is the norm.

**Carry a little cash** for the **obwarzanek** (pretzel-ring) carts on the square and traditional **milk bars (bar mleczny)**, some of which are still cash-only or have card minimums.

**ATMs:** Use bank machines (PKO BP, Santander, ING, mBank). Avoid **Euronet** machines and standalone kiosks, and always choose to be charged in **PLN**, never your home currency.

**Tipping:** 10% at sit-down restaurants is standard—round up or tell the server the total. A few coins at bars when paying each round.

connectivity.title: Staying Connected
connectivity.content:
**WiFi is standard**—cafés, restaurants, and hotels offer free access, and it's generally fast.

**EU SIMs roam free.** Non-EU visitors can grab a cheap prepaid SIM from **Play, Orange, Plus, or T-Mobile**—available at the airport, in malls, or at kiosks. Around 20-30 PLN gets you plenty of data, or use an eSIM before you arrive.

**Outlets:** Type C/E, 230V. Standard European plugs.

timing.title: Local Rhythms
timing.content:
**Kraków keeps comfortable Central-European hours**—not as late as Spain, not as early as Scandinavia.

**Restaurants:** Lunch roughly noon-3pm, dinner from 6pm; kitchens at traditional spots often wind down by 10pm. Reservations help on weekends and for places like Bottiglieria 1881.

**Shops:** 10am-7pm weekdays, shorter Saturdays. Note that many shops are **closed on non-trading Sundays** by law—check ahead, though restaurants and the Galeria Krakowska mall by the station stay open.

**Bars:** Kazimierz comes alive at night—Plac Nowy and spots like Alchemia run late, often past midnight.

**Museums:** Most open 10am-6pm and many are **closed Mondays**; some offer a free day midweek. Wawel's grounds open early and are free to wander.

**The square:** Rynek Główny at dawn is hushed and beautiful. Listen for the **hejnał** trumpet call from St. Mary's tower, played live on the hour and cut off mid-note.

quickWins.title: Insider Moves
quickWins.content:
**Rynek Główny at sunrise.** Before the tour groups arrive, the largest medieval square in Europe is nearly empty—best light, best photos, and you'll catch the **hejnał** without the crowd.

**Eat an obwarzanek from a cart, not a café.** The braided pretzel-ring is the city's classic street snack—a couple of złoty from the blue carts on the square.

**Go to Kazimierz for dinner.** The former Jewish quarter has the best food and bars—**zapiekanka** at the Plac Nowy rotunda (Endzior), Israeli food at Hamsa, and drinks at Alchemia.

**Book Auschwitz and Wieliczka ahead.** Both sell out, especially in summer—reserve timed entry online days in advance.

**Never use street exchange booths (kantor) in the Old Town.** Even '0% commission' signs hide bad rates. Use a bank ATM or your card, and always pay in PLN.

faqs.question: Is Kraków safe for tourists?
faqs.answer: Very safe overall. Petty pickpocketing can happen in crowds on Rynek Główny, on busy trams, and around the main station—keep an eye on your pockets and bag. The main scams are overpriced 'kantor' exchange booths and dynamic currency conversion at ATMs. Walking the Old Town and Kazimierz at night is generally comfortable. Emergency number is 112.

faqs.question: How much should I budget per day?
faqs.answer: Kraków is excellent value—about €60-90 per person daily covers comfortable accommodation, good meals, transport, and sights. Budget travelers can manage €40-60, partly thanks to cheap milk-bar lunches and inexpensive beer. Luxury visitors might spend €150+. A hearty meal with a drink rarely exceeds €15-20 outside fine dining; a beer is often €3-4.

faqs.question: Do I need to speak Polish?
faqs.answer: Not in tourist areas, where English is widely spoken, especially among younger people and in hospitality. A few words go a long way: 'Dzień dobry' (hello/good day), 'Dziękuję' (thank you), and 'Poproszę' (please/I'd like). Polish pronunciation is intimidating but locals appreciate any effort. Menus in the center are usually available in English.

faqs.question: Do I need to book Auschwitz and Wieliczka in advance?
faqs.answer: Yes—both should be booked ahead. The **Auschwitz-Birkenau Memorial** (in Oświęcim, ~66 km west) requires a timed entry reservation that frequently sells out, and a guided tour is mandatory during peak hours; entry to the memorial itself is free but the guide has a fee. The **Wieliczka Salt Mine** (UNESCO, ~14 km) is also guided and timed and gets very busy—reserve online a few days out, especially in summer.

faqs.question: Is Kraków tap water safe to drink?
faqs.answer: Yes—Kraków's tap water is safe and meets EU standards. It's perfectly fine to drink and refill bottles. Some older buildings have aging pipes so it can taste a bit different, but the city actively promotes drinking the tap water and has public drinking fountains in warmer months.

faqs.question: What's the best area to stay?
faqs.answer: The **Old Town (Stare Miasto)** inside the Planty puts you steps from Rynek Główny but can be pricey and lively at night. **Kazimierz**, the former Jewish quarter, is the atmospheric, foodie favorite—walkable to the center with the best bars and cafés. **Podgórze**, just across the river, is quieter and close to the Schindler Factory. Streets right on the main square are convenient but noisy.

faqs.question: How do I get around for day trips?
faqs.answer: For **Wieliczka Salt Mine**, frequent trains and buses run from near Kraków Główny in under 30 minutes. **Auschwitz-Birkenau** is reachable by direct bus or train to Oświęcim (~1.5-2 hours) or via organized tours with transport included. **Zakopane** and the Tatras (~100 km south) are served by regular buses. You don't need a car for any of these.

faqs.question: What should I definitely not miss?
faqs.answer: Rynek Główny and the Cloth Hall (Sukiennice), St. Mary's Basilica for the Veit Stoss altar and the live hejnał trumpet call, Wawel Royal Castle and Cathedral above the Vistula, and a wander through Kazimierz's synagogues and Plac Nowy. Don't skip the food: pierogi, żurek, a zapiekanka, and an obwarzanek from a street cart. With more time, the Schindler Factory museum and Rynek Underground are excellent.
